jct.source: JCT tax-expenditure (calibration target)
JCT tax-expenditure (calibration target)
JCT tax-expenditure (calibration target)
JCT tax-expenditure (calibration target)
JCT tax-expenditure (calibration target)

reforms.name: OBBBA - Reduced individual income tax rates
reforms.description: Permanent extension and limited enhancement of reduced individual income tax rates. Validated by reverting 2026 rates and brackets to the post-TCJA-expiration counterfactual.

jct.source: JCX-35-25, Ch.1 line 1 (FY2026)

reforms.name: OBBBA - Increased standard deduction
reforms.description: Extension and enhancement of the increased standard deduction. Validated by reverting 2026 standard-deduction amounts to the post-TCJA-expiration counterfactual.

jct.source: JCX-35-25, Ch.1 line 2 (FY2026)

reforms.name: OBBBA - Termination of personal exemptions other than temporary senior deduction
reforms.description: Termination of the deduction for personal exemptions other than the temporary senior deduction — the combined provision of OBBBA Sec. 70103 exactly as JCX-35-25 Ch.1 line 3 scores it (the line nets the senior deduction's cost against exemption-termination revenue). Estimate is the sum of this release's isolated increments for the two parameters (+235.4B exemptions, -21.0B senior deduction); the cross-term between them is not captured and will be from the next release, which scores the merged patch directly.

jct.source: JCX-35-25, Ch.1 line 3 (FY2026)

reforms.name: OBBBA - Increased child tax credit
reforms.description: Extension and enhancement of the increased child tax credit. Validated by reverting 2026 CTC amount, phase-out, refundable cap, phase-in threshold, and adult SSN requirement to the post-TCJA-expiration counterfactual.

jct.source: JCX-35-25, Ch.1 line 4 (FY2026)

reforms.name: OBBBA - Qualified business income deduction
reforms.description: Extension and enhancement of the qualified business income deduction. Validated by reverting the QBI deduction rates and OBBBA floor.

jct.source: JCX-35-25, Ch.1 line 5 (FY2026)

reforms.name: OBBBA - Estate and gift tax exemption
reforms.description: Extension and enhancement of increased estate and gift tax exemption amounts. Validated by reverting the 2026 estate-tax base exclusion to the post-TCJA-expiration counterfactual.

jct.source: JCX-35-25, Ch.1 line 6 (FY2026)

reforms.name: OBBBA - Alternative minimum tax parameters
reforms.description: Extension of increased AMT exemption amounts, modification of phaseout thresholds, and increased phaseout rate. Validated by reverting 2026 AMT parameters to the post-TCJA-expiration counterfactual.

jct.source: JCX-35-25, Ch.1 line 7 (FY2026)

reforms.name: OBBBA - Qualified residence interest limit
reforms.description: Extension of limitation on deduction for qualified residence interest. Validated by reverting mortgage-interest acquisition-debt caps to pre-TCJA levels. Scope note: the JCT line also includes MIP-as-qualified-residence-interest, which the reform does not model.

reforms.name: OBBBA - Casualty loss deduction limit
reforms.description: Extension and modification of limitation on casualty loss deduction. Validated by reactivating the casualty expense deduction in 2026.

jct.source: JCX-35-25, Ch.1 line 9 (FY2026)

reforms.name: OBBBA - Miscellaneous itemized deductions
reforms.description: Termination of miscellaneous itemized deductions other than educator expenses. Validated by reactivating miscellaneous itemized deductions in 2026.

reforms.name: OBBBA - Limitation on itemized deduction tax benefit
reforms.description: OBBBA limitation on the tax benefit of itemized deductions (a ~2/37 cap for top-bracket taxpayers that replaces the reinstated Pease limitation). Validated by reverting only the OBBBA cap (obbb.applies=False); limitation.applies stays True so the counterfactual is present-law Pease, and the effect is OBBBA cap minus Pease.

reforms.name: OBBBA - State and local tax deduction limit
reforms.description: Limitation on individual deductions for certain State and local taxes. Validated by reverting the SALT cap to the post-TCJA-expiration counterfactual in 2026.

reforms.name: OBBBA - No tax on tips
reforms.description: Deduction for qualified tip income. Validated by reverting the tip-income deduction cap to 0 in 2026.

jct.source: JCX-35-25, Ch.2 line 1 (FY2026)

reforms.name: OBBBA - No tax on overtime
reforms.description: Deduction for qualified overtime premium income. Validated by reverting overtime-income deduction caps to 0 in 2026.

jct.source: JCX-35-25, Ch.2 line 2 (FY2026)

reforms.name: OBBBA - No tax on car loan interest
reforms.description: Deduction for qualifying auto loan interest. Validated by reverting the auto-loan-interest deduction cap to 0 in 2026.

jct.source: JCX-35-25, Ch.2 line 3 (FY2026)

reforms.name: OBBBA - Child and dependent care tax credit
reforms.description: Enhancement of the child and dependent care tax credit. Validated by reverting 2026 CDCC rate and amended phase-out structure.

jct.source: JCX-35-25, Ch.4.A line 5 (FY2026)

reforms.name: OBBBA - Non-itemizer charitable deduction
reforms.description: Permanent and expanded reinstatement of the partial deduction for charitable contributions of individuals who do not elect to itemize. Validated by reverting 2026 non-itemizer charitable deduction amounts to 0.

jct.source: JCX-35-25, Ch.4.C line 4 (FY2026)

reforms.name: OBBBA - Charitable deduction floor
reforms.description: 0.5 percent floor on deduction of charitable contributions made by individuals. Validated by disabling the charitable donation floor in 2026.

jct.source: JCX-35-25, Ch.4.C line 5 (FY2026)

reforms.name: Child Tax Credit (incl. ODC)
reforms.description: Repeal the Child Tax Credit and Credit for Other Dependents. JCT line is 'Credit for children and other dependents'.

jct.source: JCT JCX-48-24 Table 1 (127.1B, incl. 46.7B outlay portion per footnote [4])

reforms.name: Earned Income Tax Credit
reforms.description: Repeal the EITC. Mostly refundable, so the bulk is the outlay portion. The dataset is calibrated to SOI EITC-by-AGI targets, so this is an in-sample check.

jct.source: JCT JCX-48-24 Table 1 (66.9B, incl. 57.3B outlay portion per footnote [4])

reforms.name: Child & Dependent Care Credit
reforms.description: Repeal the CDCC. JCT bundles it with the employer-provided-childcare exclusion, so the clean CDCC-only benchmark is Treasury's.

jct.source: Treasury Tax Expenditures FY2025, Table 1 item 120

reforms.name: Standard deduction (no official benchmark)
reforms.description: Repeal the standard deduction. Both JCT and Treasury treat the regular standard deduction as part of the normal-tax baseline, so there is no published tax-expenditure figure; shown as a repeal magnitude only.

jct.source: Not scored — standard deduction is baseline in both JCT and Treasury

reforms.name: All itemized deductions (no combined benchmark)
reforms.description: Repeal all itemized deductions at once. Neither JCT nor Treasury publishes a single combined itemized-deduction tax-expenditure figure (only components: SALT, mortgage, charitable, medical, …), so this carries no benchmark; the individual components are validated in-sample. Shown as a repeal magnitude only.

jct.source: Not published as a combined figure — JCT/Treasury score itemized components individually

reforms.name: GA HB1001 — income tax rate cut (5.19% → 4.99%)
reforms.description: Georgia's 5.19%→4.99% flat-rate cut for 2026, validated as a counterfactual revert. Benchmark is the DOAA note's individual-income-tax FY2028 line (-$680M); the often-quoted ~$778M OPB figure includes corporate income tax, which ga_income_tax excludes.

jct.window: FY2028 (first clean full-cut year, individual income tax only)
jct.source: Georgia DOAA/OPB revised fiscal note, HB1001 (LC 59 0226-EC), Feb 24 2026

reforms.name: MD HB411 — standard deduction increase
reforms.description: Raises Maryland's standard deduction to $4,100/$8,200 in TY2026 with COLA (one-shot, no phase-in). DLS FY2027 -$149.6M is a TY2026+partial-TY2027 catch-up year; the annualized FY2028 -$115.2M is the like-for-like single-tax-year figure. Local income tax impact excluded (md_income_tax is state-only).

jct.window: FY2028 (annualized single tax year, state GF)
jct.source: Maryland DLS fiscal note (First Reader, Feb 11 2026)

reforms.name: CO HB1062 — retirement income deduction expansion
reforms.description: Removes the cap on Colorado's pension/annuity subtraction. LCS: -$530M gross deduction impact TY2027 (-$565M steady state). Bill was postponed indefinitely; the fiscal note remains a valid benchmark.

jct.source: Colorado Legislative Council Staff fiscal note

reforms.name: ND HB1388 — marriage penalty elimination
reforms.description: Widens joint brackets to 2× single and repeals the marriage-penalty credit. Fiscal note: -$40.557M per biennium (bracket widening -$55.0M, credit repeal +$16.5M).

jct.source: ND Office of State Tax Commissioner fiscal note

reforms.name: IA HF1020 — child and dependent care credit expansion
reforms.description: Expands Iowa's CDCC fractions for higher income brackets. LSA: -$17.7M FY2026, -$16.2M FY2027; ~78,000 taxpayers affected, average decrease $228.

jct.source: Iowa LSA fiscal note

reforms.name: OK SB2156 — income tax elimination
reforms.description: Eliminates Oklahoma's personal income tax rates in 2026. Benchmark is the OTC note's own TY2026 line (-$4.947B), matching the simulation period; FY2028 steady state is -$5.30B. All-funds scope, static.

jct.source: Oklahoma Tax Commission revenue impact statement, Feb 10 2026

reforms.name: MI HB4170 — flat rate cut (4.25% → 4.05%)
reforms.description: Cuts Michigan's flat income tax rate by 0.20pp. HFA: ~$700M+/yr; 2023's actual revenue loss at 4.05% was ~$714M.

jct.source: Michigan HFA fiscal note

reforms.name: CA AB2591 — standard deduction increase
reforms.description: Raises California's standard deduction (contrib ab2591 module). FTB: -$7.0B/yr first full year, -$3.9B partial FY2026-27.

jct.source: CA FTB via Assembly Revenue & Taxation analysis

reforms.name: Repeal Minnesota CTC + Working Family Credit
reforms.description: Combined credits (shared phase-out); benchmark is the House Research combined cost.

jct.source: MN House Research, May 2026, p.1

reforms.name: Repeal California CalEITC
reforms.description: One-year vintage gap: TY2023 actual vs the 2024 dataset period.

jct.source: CA FTB EITC/YCTC/FYTC Report (Oct 2025), Table 1 p.8

reforms.name: Repeal California Young Child Tax Credit

jct.source: CA FTB EITC/YCTC/FYTC Report (Oct 2025), Table 1 p.8

reforms.name: Repeal New York EITC
reforms.description: NYS EITC only; the NYC and noncustodial credits are separate variables.

jct.source: NYS DTF open data, EITC Claims Table 3

reforms.name: Repeal New York Empire State Child Credit
reforms.description: TY2023 and the 2024 dataset period share the regular 33%-of-federal structure.

jct.source: NYS DTF open data, ESCC Study

reforms.name: Repeal Colorado EITC
reforms.description: TY2023 and the 2024 model year both use a 50% match; the separately reported $23.1M Expanded COEITC is excluded from the benchmark.

jct.source: CO DOR ORA, Individual Income Tax Credits TY2023
CO DOR ORA, Individual Income Tax Credits TY2023
CT DRS Annual Report FY2024-25, p.35

reforms.name: Repeal New Mexico Working Families Tax Credit

jct.source: 2025 NM Tax Expenditure Report, p.259

reforms.name: Repeal New Mexico Child Income Tax Credit

jct.source: 2025 NM Tax Expenditure Report, p.81

reforms.name: Repeal Maryland EITC
reforms.description: Benchmark sums the refundable ($410.1M) and nonrefundable ($140.8M) lines; repealing the take-better-of credit removes both.

jct.source: Comptroller of Maryland, Income Tax Summary Report TY2024, pp.18-23

reforms.name: Repeal Illinois EITC
reforms.description: Illinois-resident returns; 20% match in both years.

jct.source: IDOR TY2023 final stratification, p.1

reforms.name: Total income tax (net of refundable credits)
reforms.description: SOI total income tax minus refundable credits — the same net concept as populace's income_tax. Small known scope difference: the populace measure also includes qualified-retirement-plan penalty taxes and unreported payroll tax (~0.5% of the total), which SOI books outside income tax.

jct.source: IRS SOI Pub 1304 TY2023, Table 3.3 (total income tax minus refundable credits)

reforms.name: Alternative minimum tax
reforms.description: Post-TCJA AMT is small (about 150,000 returns in TY2023), so this line is sensitive to the top tail of the income distribution.

jct.source: IRS SOI Pub 1304 TY2023, Table 1.4 (alternative minimum tax)

reforms.name: Child and dependent care credit
reforms.description: SOI reports the credit amount used to offset tax; the populace side is min(credit available, income tax before credits) per tax unit — an approximation that ignores IRS credit ordering, so populace remains a slight upper bound.

jct.source: IRS SOI Pub 1304 TY2023, Table 3.3 (credit for child and dependent care)

reforms.name: Education credits (nonrefundable)
reforms.description: populace's education_tax_credits is the nonrefundable American Opportunity credit plus the Lifetime Learning credit — the same scope as SOI's nonrefundable education credit column (the refundable AOTC portion is excluded on both sides). SOI reports the credit amount used to offset tax; the populace side is min(credit available, income tax before credits) per tax unit — an approximation that ignores IRS credit ordering, so populace remains a slight upper bound.

jct.source: IRS SOI Pub 1304 TY2023, Table 3.3 (nonrefundable education credit)

reforms.name: Retirement savings contribution (saver's) credit
reforms.description: SOI reports the credit amount used to offset tax; the populace side is min(credit available, income tax before credits) per tax unit — an approximation that ignores IRS credit ordering, so populace remains a slight upper bound.

jct.source: IRS SOI Pub 1304 TY2023, Table 3.3 (retirement savings contribution credit)

reforms.name: Child tax credit + ODC (nonrefundable)
reforms.description: SOI reports the credit amount used to offset tax; the populace side is min(credit available, income tax before credits) per tax unit — an approximation that ignores IRS credit ordering, so populace remains a slight upper bound.

jct.source: IRS SOI Pub 1304 TY2023, Table 3.3 (nonrefundable child and other dependent tax credit)
IRS SOI Pub 1304 TY2023, Table 3.3 (refundable child tax credit or additional child tax credit)

reforms.name: Net investment income tax
reforms.description: Concentrated in top-decile capital income, so this line tests the investment-income tail.

jct.source: IRS SOI Pub 1304 TY2023, Table 3.3 (net investment income tax)
IRS SOI Pub 1304 TY2023, Table 3.3 (self-employment tax)

reforms.name: California CalEITC
reforms.description: Refundable; 3,340,639 returns, avg $282. Tax-year basis (process-year basis is $986M). TY2024 report not yet published, so a one-year vintage gap applies.

jct.source: CA FTB, California EITC / YCTC / FYTC Report (Oct 2025), Table 1 p.8

reforms.name: California Young Child Tax Credit
reforms.description: Refundable; 398,059 returns, 483,629 qualifying children.

jct.source: CA FTB, California EITC / YCTC / FYTC Report (Oct 2025), Table 1 p.8

reforms.name: New York EITC
reforms.description: Refundable, 30% of federal less the household credit; 1,435,448 claims. NYS EITC only - the separate NYC and noncustodial EITCs are excluded from both sides.

jct.source: NYS DTF open data, EITC Claims by Place of Residence, Table 3 (Grand Total)

reforms.name: New York Empire State Child Credit
reforms.description: Refundable; 1,451,132 claims, 2,429,901 qualifying children. TY2023 and the 2024 dataset period both use the regular 33%-of-federal structure with the under-4 expansion; the age-based restructure begins 2025. Excludes the one-time 2024 supplemental checks.

jct.source: NYS DTF open data, Empire State Child Credit Study by Place of Residence

reforms.name: Colorado EITC
reforms.description: Refundable; 311,343 claims, avg $1,145. TY2023 used a one-year 50% match and the 2024 model year is also 50% (HB24-1134), so the rates align. A further $23.1M Expanded COEITC (ITIN and under-25 filers) is reported separately; populace models the under-25 expansion, so its total may sit between the two figures.

jct.source: CO DOR Office of Research and Analysis, Individual Income Tax Credits TY2023 (updated Mar 2026)

reforms.name: Colorado Child Tax Credit
reforms.description: Refundable; 130,188 claims, avg $685. Children under 6, 10-60% of the federal CTC by AGI tier.

jct.source: CO DOR Office of Research and Analysis, Individual Income Tax Credits TY2023 (updated Mar 2026)

reforms.name: Washington Working Families Tax Credit
reforms.description: 284,416 approved applications as of Oct 2025. Claims may be filed up to three years late, so the published total still grows; populace, which assumes take-up among modeled eligibles, should sit at or above it.

jct.source: WA DOR, WFTC Program 2025 Report to the Legislature (Dec 2025), Tables 2 and 4

reforms.name: Minnesota Child Tax Credit + Working Family Credit
reforms.description: The two credits share one phase-out and populace computes them as one combined variable. House Research combined cost: 240,100 returns with children (avg $2,773) plus 301,500 childless returns (avg $297). MN DOR separately reports the CTC share as $564M across 219k families and 447k children.

jct.source: MN House Research, Minnesota Child Credit and Working Family Credit (May 2026), p.1

reforms.name: Minnesota Working Family Credit
reforms.description: No standalone official WFC total is published; this subtracts two official figures from different sources, so treat as approximate. The childless-return subtotal ($89.6M) is a clean official lower bound.

jct.source: Derived: House Research combined CTC+WFC $755.5M minus MN DOR CTC $564M (both TY2024)

reforms.name: Connecticut EITC
reforms.description: Fully refundable, 40% of federal; 193,283 credits. The $250-per-filer qualifying-child supplement begins TY2025 and is excluded from both sides.

jct.source: CT DRS Annual Report FY2024-25, p.35

reforms.name: New Mexico Working Families Tax Credit
reforms.description: Refundable, 25% of federal; 201,819 claims.

jct.source: 2025 New Mexico Tax Expenditure Report (TRD), p.259 (reliability 1: separately reported actuals)

reforms.name: New Mexico Child Income Tax Credit
reforms.description: Refundable, $25-$600 per child stepping down with AGI; 243,909 claiming returns.

jct.source: 2025 New Mexico Tax Expenditure Report (TRD), p.81 (reliability 1)

reforms.name: Rhode Island EITC
reforms.description: Refundable; 73,463 taxpayers. TY2023 used the 15% match; the 2024 model year is 16%, so populace should run roughly 7% above (ORA projects TY2024 at $29.8M).

jct.source: 2026 RI Tax Expenditures Report (Office of Revenue Analysis, Jan 2026), Credits item 8, p.77

reforms.name: Illinois EITC
reforms.description: Refundable, 20% of federal in both TY2023 and the 2024 model year; 899,078 Illinois-resident returns. The all-filer total including non-IL addresses is $451.0M; populace models residents.

jct.source: IDOR, Individual Income Tax Returns by AGI, TY2023 final stratification (Nov 2025), p.1

reforms.name: Hawaii EITC
reforms.description: Refundable, 40% of federal; 78,399 new-credit claims. Excludes pre-2023 nonrefundable-credit carryover the report also counts.

jct.source: HI DOTAX, Earned Income Tax Credit Report TY2024 (Act 107 report, Dec 2025), Table 1 p.5

reforms.name: Iowa EITC
reforms.description: Refundable, 15% of federal. FY2025 actual claims basis.

jct.source: IA DOR, Tax Credits Contingent Liabilities Report (Mar 2026), Tables 2 and 8

reforms.name: Kansas EITC
reforms.description: Refundable, 17% of federal; 171,232 claims (process year 2024, i.e. TY2023 returns).

jct.source: KS DOR, Tax Expenditure Report CY2024, Earned Income Credit (K.S.A. 79-32,205), p.9

reforms.name: Louisiana EITC
reforms.description: Refundable, 5% of federal. Fiscal-year revenue-loss basis (FYE June 2025, mostly TY2024 returns).

jct.source: LA DOR, Tax Exemption Budget 2025-2026, item 78, p.38

reforms.name: Nebraska EITC
reforms.description: Refundable, 10% of federal; 111,150 claims. Residents only.

jct.source: NE DOR, Nebraska Statistics of Individual Income Tax, Table F2 2024 (refundable credits)

reforms.name: Wisconsin Earned Income Credit
reforms.description: Refundable, 4/11/34% of federal by number of children (childless ineligible); 193,799 claims, avg $453.

jct.source: WI Legislative Fiscal Bureau, Updated Tax Information for 2025 LFB Informational Papers (Apr 2025), Tables 8-10

reforms.name: Vermont EITC
reforms.description: Refundable, 38% of federal in both TY2022 and the 2024 model year (the childless rate rises to 100% only from TY2025); 33,520 returns. Oldest benchmark in this file - a two-year vintage gap applies.

jct.source: 2025 Vermont Tax Expenditures Biennial Report (JFO and Dept of Taxes, Jan 2025), Appendix C p.77

reforms.name: Vermont Child Tax Credit
reforms.description: Refundable, $1,000 per child under 6 with a 2% per $1,000 phase-out above $125k AGI; 21,296 returns, 27,214 children. Age limit rises to under 7 only from TY2025.

jct.source: 2025 Vermont Tax Expenditures Biennial Report, Appendix C p.77; VT JFO one-year lookback brief (Dec 2023)

reforms.name: Maryland EITC (refundable + nonrefundable)
reforms.description: Sum of two official lines: $410.1M refunded EIC (361,865 returns) plus $140.8M nonrefundable EIC claimed against liability (246,823 returns) - populace md_eitc pays whichever of the two credits is larger per filer, matching the summed concept. The local EITC is netted out of local tax and never published.

jct.source: Comptroller of Maryland, Income Tax Summary Report TY2024, refund summary pp.18-19 and selected credits pp.21-23

reforms.name: Oregon EITC
reforms.description: Refundable, 9% of federal (12% with a child under 3); 212,930 full-year-resident returns including ITIN filers. Full-year residents only - all-filer totals run 6-7% higher, so populace should land at or slightly above.

jct.source: TY2023 Oregon Personal Income Tax Statistics (150-101-406, revised), Exhibits 36 and 38

reforms.name: Oregon Kids Credit
reforms.description: Refundable, $1,000 per child 0-5 with a $25k-$30k MAGI phase-out; 30,110 full-year-resident returns, avg $1,140. DOR reported over $39M across all filers; official take-up is roughly half of eligible families, while populace assumes full take-up - expect populace above the benchmark.

jct.source: TY2023 Oregon Personal Income Tax Statistics (150-101-406, revised), Exhibit 36

reforms.name: Maryland Child Tax Credit
reforms.description: Refundable $500 per child under 6 (or disabled under 17) for AGI under $15k. FY2024 reflects TY2023, the first expanded year; the report's EITC lines match tax-year actuals exactly, so the figure is claims-based. No claim counts published.

jct.source: Maryland Tax Expenditures Report FY2024 (DBM Bureau of Revenue Estimates), pp.23 and 47

reforms.name: New Jersey EITC
reforms.description: Refundable, 40% of federal. Total computed from printed claims and average (total not printed), so treat as approximate; the report projects $628.1M for FY2027.

jct.source: NJ Tax Expenditure Report FY2027 (Treasury/Taxation), item 40 p.33: 617,800 claims x $977 average

reforms.name: New Jersey Child Tax Credit
reforms.description: Refundable, $200-$1,000 per child under 6 by income tier up to $80k. Total computed from printed claims and average.

jct.source: NJ Tax Expenditure Report FY2027 (Treasury/Taxation), item 63 p.44: 232,500 credits x $949 average

reforms.name: Massachusetts EITC
reforms.description: Refundable, 40% of federal from TY2023. Fiscal-year tax-expenditure estimate (no claim counts published); FY2025 estimate is $341.4M.

jct.source: MA FY26 Tax Expenditure Budget, item 1.605

reforms.name: Massachusetts Child and Family Tax Credit
reforms.description: Refundable, uncapped $440 per dependent from TY2024. The FY2025 estimate reflects the $440 rate that also applies in the 2024 model year (FY2024 reflected the first-year $310 rate).

jct.source: MA FY26 Tax Expenditure Budget, item 1.628

reforms.name: Maine EITC
reforms.description: Refundable, 25% of federal (50% for childless filers); roughly 92,000 taxpayers. Microsimulation-based fiscal-year revenue-loss estimate, net of a TANF-fund offset.

jct.source: Maine State Tax Expenditure Report 2026-2027 (MRS Office of Tax Policy), item 1.032 p.36

reforms.name: Michigan EITC
reforms.description: Refundable, 30% of federal. Total implied from the printed claim count and average (Treasury prints no total), so treat as approximate.

jct.source: MI Treasury press releases (Oct 2025, Apr 2024): about 665,000 claims at an $890 average

reforms.name: Montana EITC
reforms.description: Montana's match rose from 3% to 10% in TY2024; the only official actual ($3.9M, DOR biennial report Table 3.13) is from the 3% era, so the IRS-derived figure is the fairer current-rate benchmark.

jct.source: Approximation: 10% statutory match x $156.1M federal EITC claimed in MT (IRS EITC Central, TY2024 returns processed in 2025)

reforms.name: Indiana EITC
reforms.description: Refundable, 10% of federal under Indiana-specific qualifying rules. FY2024 LSA estimate (latest actual is FY2022 at $77.5M, before the 10% recoupling).

jct.source: 2024 Indiana Tax Expenditure Review (LSA OFMA, Oct 2024), item IT42 p.21

reforms.name: Oklahoma EITC
reforms.description: Refundable, 5% of federal computed under frozen pre-2021 federal parameters, so an IRS-derived approximation is invalid here; the official TY2022 actual (321,693 claims) is the best available despite the two-year vintage gap.

jct.source: Oklahoma Tax Commission, Tax Expenditure Report 2023-2024, income tax credits item 13, p.6 (reliability level 1)

reforms.name: Delaware EITC
reforms.description: Filers choose a 20% nonrefundable or 4.5% refundable credit; Delaware publishes only an estimated fiscal-year revenue loss (FY2026 estimate: $18.0-18.5M), with no claim counts.

jct.source: 2025 Delaware Tax Preference Report, Personal Income Tax chapter section 1.16, p.1-28

reforms.name: District of Columbia EITC
reforms.description: Refundable; ORA revenue-loss estimate at the 70% match in force for TY2022-2024 (the match rises to 100% in TY2025). Latest published actual is TY2021 ($69.6M at the 40% match). Credits of $1,200+ are paid in monthly installments, blurring fiscal-year timing.

jct.source: DC Tax Expenditure Report (OCFO/ORA, Sept 2024), item 128 p.235

reforms.name: Illinois Child Tax Credit
reforms.description: Refundable, 20% of the filer's Illinois EITC (child under 12) in TY2024, doubling to 40% for TY2025+. GOMB statewide estimate - no official actuals or claim counts published yet.

jct.source: Illinois State Budget FY2027 (GOMB, Feb 2026), p.28

reforms.name: Arizona Dependent Tax Credit
reforms.description: About 1.49M Arizonans under 17 x $100 plus roughly 99k 17-year-olds x $25. Upper bound: the credit phases out above $200k single / $400k joint AGI, and 18+ dependents (omitted here) partially offset that.

jct.source: Approximation: ACS 2024 1-year age counts for Arizona x statutory amounts ($100 per dependent under 17, $25 at 17+)

reforms.name: Idaho Grocery Credit
reforms.description: Upper bound: the credit is prorated by months not receiving SNAP and excludes incarcerated months, and the seniors add-on ($20, in force through 2024) is omitted; expect populace below the benchmark.

jct.source: Approximation: ACS 2024 1-year Idaho population (2,001,619) x the $120 per-person credit

_backfill_note: State reform rows appended offline (PolicyEngine/populace#319 suite) by scoring the released populace_us_2024.h5 at policyengine-us 1.729.0; the release itself was built at 1.752.2, so minor state-tax logic drift is possible. All other rows are the published artifact, untouched. | 2026-07-06 benchmark audit: mortgage FY2027 3,398M->3,110M (wrong-line transcription), GA -778M->-680M (corporate excluded), MD -149.6M->-115.2M (catch-up FY), OK -5.3B->-4.947B (TY2026 line); all other 94 external values verified against primary sources. | MA H5007 removed: benchmark exists only as press paraphrase, no primary source (suite policy: official published notes only). | 2026-07-06 line-3 scope fix: exemption row now scores the combined Sec. 70103 scope (termination + senior deduction, matching the JCX line); standalone senior row removed. Note: this release's OBBBA effects were scored in isolation against pre-OBBBA law (all rows share the 2,735.78B baseline), not stacked; the stacked producer applies from the next release.
